name: Vocabulary: Idioms, Phrases, and Phrasal Verbs

sourceRefs.label: 2025_q1 page 17 Q29 (4 phrasal verbs fill-in-the-blank, with answer key)
2022_q page 23 Q85; 2024_q page 87 Q181-182 (idiom/phrase meanings, with answer key)
2024_q page 84 Q171 (stashed earlier); 2025_q1 page 37 Q82 (stashed earlier, idiom-pair swap trap)

researchNotes: Second Vocabulary topic, consuming both idiom-related facts stashed since early in the project plus 3 fresh hits. Real exam evidence favours testing idiomatic MEANING (multiple-choice 'what does this phrase mean') over recall, and phrasal verbs via fill-in-the-blank with a shared word bank.

reading.intro: Covers common idioms/phrases tested by their MEANING (not just recognition) and phrasal verbs via fill-in-the-blank — exactly the formats real exams use.

qa.question: Real exam: Fill in these phrasal verbs — "ring her ___" (call), "drop ___" (visit informally), "do ___ it" (manage without), "trying it ___" (testing) — using out/in/up/without.
qa.answer: Ring her UP (call her). Drop IN (visit casually). Do WITHOUT it (manage without having it). Trying it OUT (testing it) — all four confirmed via a real exam's fill-in-the-blank question using a shared word bank.

qa.question: Real exam: What does the phrase "given to" mean?
qa.answer: Accustomed to (e.g., 'he is given to exaggeration' = he is accustomed to/has a habit of exaggerating) — confirmed by a real exam question.

qa.question: Real exam: What does the phrase "cast down" mean?
qa.answer: Dejected (sad, downcast) — confirmed by a real exam question.

qa.question: Real exam: What does the idiom/saying "failures are stepping stones to success" mean, and what does the phrase "reconcile to" mean?
qa.answer: "Failures are stepping stones to success" means setbacks/failures are necessary steps that lead toward eventual success, not permanent obstacles — confirmed by a real exam question. "Reconcile to" means to ACCEPT (something unwelcome) half-heartedly/reluctantly, not to fully embrace it — also confirmed by a real exam question.

qa.question: Real exam: What does the idiom "beauty lies in the eyes of the beholder" mean?
qa.answer: What is considered beautiful depends on the individual perceiving it — beauty is subjective, not an objective, universal standard.

qa.question: Real exam: What is the REAL meaning of "to be at large", and of "to pass the buck" — and what is the common trap?
qa.answer: "To be at large" really means FREE / AT LIBERTY (e.g., a criminal still 'at large' has not been captured). "To pass the buck" really means TO BLAME (someone else) / SHIFT RESPONSIBILITY onto another person. A real exam tested these two idioms with their meanings deliberately SWAPPED with each other as a trap — don't assume the more 'obvious-sounding' pairing is correct.

questions.prompt: "You'd better ring her ___ and tell her you'll be late."
"I invited her to drop ___ any time she was passing for a cup of tea."
"If you can't afford it, you'll have to do ___ it."
"He won't buy the car without trying it ___ on the road first."
What does the phrase "given to" mean?
What does the phrase "cast down" mean?
What does the phrase "reconcile to" mean?
What does the idiom "to be at large" really mean?
What does the idiom "to pass the buck" really mean?
options: To shift blame/responsibility onto someone else

questions.prompt: "Beauty lies in the eyes of the beholder" means:
options: Beauty is always objective and universal
Beauty is subjective — it depends on who is looking
Only beautiful people can judge beauty
Beauty cannot be perceived by the eyes
